summary: In this JAMA abstract reporting on the Women's Health Initiative randomized trials, JoAnn E. Manson and colleagues conclude that menopausal hormone therapy (estrogen plus progestin or estrogen alone) taken for 5 to 7 years is not associated with an increased risk of long-term all-cause, cardiovascular, or cancer mortality in postmenopausal women over an 18-year follow-up.

principles.statement: Menopausal hormone therapy used for 5 to 7 years does not increase the long-term risk of mortality in postmenopausal women.
principles.rationale: An 18-year cumulative follow-up of the Women's Health Initiative randomized trials demonstrated no significant association between hormone therapy (either CEE plus MPA or CEE alone) and all-cause, cardiovascular, or cancer mortality.
principles.source_quote: Among postmenopausal women, hormone therapy with CEE plus MPA for a median of 5.6 years or with CEE alone for a median of 7.2 years was not associated with risk of all-cause, cardiovascular, or cancer mortality during a cumulative follow-up of 18 years.

signature_quotes.text: Among postmenopausal women, hormone therapy with CEE plus MPA for a median of 5.6 years or with CEE alone for a median of 7.2 years was not associated with risk of all-cause, cardiovascular, or cancer mortality during a cumulative follow-up of 18 years.
signature_quotes.context: The primary conclusion drawn from the 18-year cumulative follow-up of the Women's Health Initiative randomized trials.
